insights: Critical-priority expense reports take 24% longer to process than Low-priority reports (median 2971 vs 2247 hours), directly contradicting the intended efficiency logic of the priority system and indicating a systemic misalignment between cost proxy and processing speed.

The priority system appears misconfigured rather than absent: Priority 2-High consistently shows the fastest processing time across most categories, suggesting that mid-tier priority assignments may unintentionally trigger more efficient routing or resource allocation.

Category-based processing times show only modest disparity (1.44x range: Software at 2157 hours vs Database at 3114 hours), indicating relatively equitable treatment across functional expense types despite the priority inefficiency.

Timestamp inversion affected 52.8% of records but was randomly distributed across all dimensions (priority, category, location, assignee), confirming it was a data quality artifact—not a structural bias—so the corrected processing times reliably reflect operational reality.

All 500 expense reports now have valid, non-negative processing times ranging from 1.95 to 9197 hours (median ~2636 hours), enabling confident interpretation of priority and category effects on processing duration.
summary: The analysis directly addresses the goal of understanding how expense cost—proxied by priority—affects processing time, revealing a critical contradiction: higher-priority (i.e., presumed higher-cost) expenses are processed *slower*, not faster, with Critical-priority reports taking 24% longer than Low-priority ones. This indicates the current priority mechanism undermines both efficiency and equity objectives. While category-based disparities are modest (1.44x), the consistent outperformance of Priority 2-High suggests the priority logic may be inverted or misaligned with actual resource allocation rules. The timestamp correction was fully successful and unbiased, validating the reliability of these findings. Next, focus should shift to auditing priority assignment rules and investigating why Priority 2-High triggers faster resolution—this is the highest-leverage opportunity to realign the system with its intended cost-sensitive efficiency goals.
